planting.note: Divide clumps or detach and pot the plantlets that form at the flower-stalk tips, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; walking iris is one of the finest and most reliable shade-tolerant flowering perennials for zone 9–12 and blooms intermittently throughout the warm season in quite deep shade conditions.

directions: Divide clumps or pot up rooted plantlets from stem tips in moist, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; walking iris (Apostle Plant) is a Brazilian tropical evergreen perennial of the iris family (Iridaceae) that is one of the most valuable and widely used shade-tolerant flowering plants for Gulf Coast and tropical gardens — producing beautiful, iris-like, white flowers with purple and brown markings on upright, flat, sword-like stems that resemble those of a large iris, blooming intermittently through the warm season in partial to full shade conditions. The common name 'walking iris' refers to the plant's ingenious self-propagating method: after flowering, the flower stalk bends under the weight of a new plantlet (a miniature plant complete with leaves and roots) that forms at the stalk tip and, when the stalk touches the ground, the plantlet roots in and starts a new plant — 'walking' the colony progressively outward from the parent. This gives it the ability to spread and colonize shade garden areas without any gardener intervention. An excellent, easy-to-grow, and self-spreading shade groundcover for zone 9–12 tropical shade gardens. Blooms most freely with about 13 leaves present — hence 'Apostle Plant'.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Panama rose requires acidic soil and reacts to alkalinity with iron deficiency chlorosis — supplement with chelated iron or soil acidifiers if yellowing between the veins occurs.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Panama rose is a Cuban and Central American tropical evergreen shrub in the coffee family (Rubiaceae) that produces rounded clusters of small, tubular, brilliant coral-orange to red-orange, five-lobed, fragrant flowers with a yellow eye, borne in profusion at the shoot tips from late spring through fall and intermittently at other seasons — the vivid coral-orange color and sweet fragrance combine to create an attractive and useful shade-tolerant flowering shrub for zone 9–11 gardens. The glossy, dark-green, oval, wrinkled leaves create an attractive foliage backdrop for the bright flowers. Panama rose is shade-tolerant for a tropical flowering shrub and performs acceptably in partial-shade conditions where most comparable orange-flowered shrubs would fail to bloom. It requires acidic soil and is sensitive to alkalinity. In zone 9 it is often damaged or killed by hard freezes but may regrow from established roots. An underused but excellent shade-tolerant flowering shrub for the Gulf Coast.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings under mist or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; mussaenda flowers most prolifically in the warmest, most humid months and benefits from regular fertilization throughout the growing season to maintain vigor and continuous flower production.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; tropical dogwood (Ashanti blood, red flag bush) is a West African tropical shrub in the coffee family (Rubiaceae) that creates one of the most spectacular and unusual flowering effects available in the tropical garden — the small, true yellow flowers are relatively inconspicuous, but each is surrounded by one or more greatly enlarged, modified, leaf-like, blood-red to rose-red calyx lobes (sepals) that function as the visual attractor, and in full flowering these large, papery, brilliantly colored bracts cover the entire plant in a mass of vivid red-orange or pink that resembles a bougainvillea in intensity. The Philippine white mussaenda (Mussaenda philippica) is a white-bracted form of similar effect. The bracts are produced most abundantly through the warm season. All parts of the plant are reportedly toxic if ingested. In zone 10–12 it grows as a large, evergreen, freely-branching shrub; in zone 9 it may be grown as a container specimen moved indoors in winter. An extraordinary tropical specimen for zone 10–12 gardens.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Chinese ixora is considerably more shade-tolerant than the common tropical ixora (Ixora coccinea) and performs in the dappled light of open tree canopy where the red-flowered species would cease blooming.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Chinese ixora is an East Asian evergreen shrub of the coffee family (Rubiaceae) closely related to the common tropical ixora (I. coccinea) but producing its characteristic, dense, rounded, terminal clusters of small, tubular flowers in a soft-pink to rose-pink or white color rather than the vivid red-orange of the common species. The flowers are produced continuously from spring through fall, with some intermittent bloom at other seasons in frost-free climates, creating a long-season display of soft-pink color that is more easily incorporated into refined garden color schemes than the intense red-orange of common ixora. Chinese ixora is notably more shade-tolerant than its common relative, blooming acceptably in partial shade conditions — making it one of the few continuously-blooming flowering shrubs useful in shaded positions in zone 9–12. Like all ixoras, it requires strongly acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) for best performance; alkaline soil causes interveinal chlorosis. It is more cold-hardy than I. coccinea, surviving temperatures to approximately 28°F in zone 9.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Mexican blood trumpet produces its most prolific flowering in full sun but blooms acceptably in partial shade — it is one of the better shade-tolerant bignonia-family vines for zone 9–12.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; Mexican blood trumpet is a Mexican evergreen vine of the bignonia family that produces large, striking, trumpet-shaped flowers in a dramatic two-toned color combination — the flowers open from orange-red buds into large, flared trumpets in a vivid blood-red to orange-red outer color that fades inside through orange to a yellow throat, creating a warm, vivid, tricolored trumpet effect. The flowers are produced in dense clusters at the branch tips from spring through fall, with the primary flush in spring and a second in fall. The vine climbs by tendrils and can reach 20–30 feet on suitable support. It is more shade-tolerant than Pyrostegia venusta and blooms more reliably in partial-shade conditions, making it a good choice for lightly shaded arbors and structures where the flame vine would fail to produce its best display. Hardy to approximately 25–28°F in zone 9, with established plants typically recovering from frost damage by regrowing from the base. An excellent flowering vine for large arbors, pergolas, and walls in zone 9–12.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
